did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on over 28,000 dermoscopic images to produce a malignancy score and assign a traffic light system (green, yellow, red) for dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis. This AI system was used to assist 64 physicians in a retrospective reader study, resulting in improved diagnostic sensitivity and specificity compared to using images alone.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is titled 'Combining artificial intelligence and human expertise for more accurate dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis: A 2-session retrospective reader study' and describes original experimental work involving a CNN trained on 28,000 images and a reader study with 64 physicians. It reports new results on diagnostic sensitivity and specificity improvements, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a Research Letter).

title: Combining artificial intelligence and human expertise for more accurate dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis: A 2-session retrospective reader study
brief_summary: This retrospective reader study evaluated the impact of combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with human expertise for dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis. The CNN, trained on over 28,000 dermoscopic images, generated a malignancy score and a traffic light system (green, yellow, red) to assist 64 physicians. Participants evaluated a challenging test set of 100 dermoscopic images in two sessions: first based on images alone, and second with CNN assistance. The results showed that diagnostic sensitivity and specificity significantly improved from 56.31% and 69.28% to 67.88% and 73.72%, respectively, when using the AI aid. The study concludes that collaborative utilization of CNNs alongside clinical expertise enhances diagnostic accuracy in melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as part of the test set (50 melanomas) and the CNN holdout training/validation set (344 melanomas). Classified as malignant in the traffic light system.
Studied as part of the test set (50 nevi) and the CNN holdout training/validation set (2155 nevi). Classified as benign in the traffic light system.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy status (benign vs malignant) of dermoscopic lesions, output as a malignancy score (0 to 1) and a traffic light system (green, yellow, red).
all_task_types.details: The CNN was trained to classify dermoscopic images as benign (nevi) or malignant (melanomas). It produced a malignancy score from 0 to 1. Based on performance on a holdout test set, cutoffs were determined to assign traffic light colors: Green (<0.65) for benign, Yellow (0.65 to 0.75) for intermediate risk, and Red (>0.75) for malignant. The study reports sensitivity and specificity, indicating a binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: CNN Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermoscopic images used to train the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The specific source institution is not named in the text, but the study was conducted at IDI-IRCCS.

use.training: Used to train the CNN to produce a malignancy score from 0 to 1.

datasets_used.name: CNN Holdout Control Test Set
datasets_used.description: A previously unseen holdout test set used to determine performance metrics and cutoffs for the CNN's traffic light system. It comprised images from patients aged 18 to 84 with Fitzpatrick skin types I to IV, excluding mucosa and nails.

use.validation: Used to determine cutoffs for the traffic light system (Green <0.65, Yellow 0.65-0.75, Red >0.75) and to establish baseline CNN sensitivity (88%) and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Reader Study Test Set
datasets_used.description: A deliberately challenging test set of 100 dermoscopic images used in a 2-session retrospective reader study involving 64 physicians. Images were from 61 male and 39 female patients aged 18 to 84 with Fitzpatrick skin types I to IV, encompassing all body sites (excluding mucosa and nails). Histopathological results were available for all images.

use.testing: Used in Session 1 (images alone) and Session 2 (with CNN assistance) to evaluate diagnostic sensitivity and specificity of 64 physicians.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the CNN on the holdout test set and the diagnostic performance of 64 physicians in Session 1 (images alone) and Session 2 (with CNN assistance).
To evaluate the discriminative ability of the CNN on the holdout control test set.
To provide interpretational clarity and demonstrate the trade-off between minimizing false negatives and false positives for the CNN-assigned traffic light system cutoffs.
To aggregate the diagnostic sensitivity and specificity scores across the 64 physicians for both study sessions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper reports mean diagnostic sensitivity and specificity values for physicians with and without AI assistance and notes that these values 'improved,' but it does not report any formal statistical hypothesis tests (such as p-values, confidence intervals, or specific test names like McNemar's test) to determine if this improvement was statistically significant.

CNN Holdout Control Test Set.notes: Based on performance on the holdout test set (2489 images: 2155 nevi, 344 melanomas), cutoffs for the traffic light system were determined. Implied predicted values: Green (<0.65) - 98% of lesions confirmed benign; Yellow (0.65-0.75) - 25% of lesions confirmed malignant; Red (>0.75) - 70% of lesions confirmed malignant.

Reader Study Test Set (Session 1 - Image.notes: Participants evaluated 100 dermoscopic images (50 melanomas, 50 nevi) based solely on images.

Reader Study Test Set (Session 2 - With.notes: Participants evaluated the same 100 images with assistance of the CNN-assigned traffic light system. Results show improved sensitivity and specificity compared to Session 1.

architecture_details.details: The specific architecture name (e.g., ResNet, VGG) is not reported. The model was trained on over 28,000 dermoscopic images.

classification_or_output_head.post_processing: Traffic light system assignment based on cutoffs: Green (<0.65), Yellow (0.65-0.75), Red (>0.75)

preprocessing_techniques: Data anonymization (deidentification of photographs and medical information)
Exclusion of images from mucosa and nails
conclusions: Combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with human expertise significantly improved diagnostic sensitivity (from 56.31% to 67.88%) and specificity (from 69.28% to 73.72%) in a retrospective reader study of 64 physicians evaluating a challenging test set of 100 dermoscopic images.
The CNN, trained on over 28,000 dermoscopic images, achieved 88% sensitivity and specificity on a holdout control test set, with an area under the curve of 0.94.
The study supports the collaborative utilization of CNNs alongside clinical expertise to enhance diagnostic accuracy in dermoscopic melanoma diagnosis.

The CNN-assisted traffic light system (green, yellow, red) provides interpretational clarity through implied predicted values, demonstrating a deliberate trade-off between minimizing false negatives and false positives.
important_remarks: The study is a Research Letter with a small, deliberately challenging test set of only 100 images (50 melanomas, 50 nevi), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the reader study results.

The paper does not report p-values, confidence intervals, or formal statistical hypothesis tests to confirm the significance of the improvement in sensitivity (56.31% to 67.88%) and specificity (69.28% to 73.72%) observed in the reader study.

There is a significant time gap of 41 months between Session 1 (images alone) and Session 2 (with CNN assistance), introducing potential confounding factors such as physician learning effects or changes in clinical practice over time.

The CNN was trained on over 28,000 images, but the specific source institution for the training data is not named, raising potential concerns about dataset bias and external validation beyond the IDI-IRCCS context.

The study population for the reader test set was restricted to Fitzpatrick skin types I to IV, excluding mucosa and nails, which limits the applicability of the findings to darker skin types (Fitzpatrick V-VI) and non-cutaneous sites.

The CNN's performance metrics (88% sensitivity/specificity) were derived from a holdout control test set, but the specific architecture details, hyperparameters, and training methodology of the CNN are not reported.
The study design is retrospective, which may introduce selection bias compared to prospective, real-world clinical deployment scenarios.
